title_en: Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj: Al-Kasani's Systematic Framework
content_en:
Al-Kasani's treatment of zakat, fasting, and hajj in Bada'i as-Sana'i applies his systematic conditions-pillars-invalidating factors framework to these three pillars of Islam, producing the clearest available organizational account of Hanafi positions on these foundational obligations.

For zakat, al-Kasani identifies the conditions that make the obligation arise (shurut al-wujub): Islam, freedom, sanity, nisab-level wealth, and the hawl. He then identifies what the zakat obligation itself consists of (what must be paid, to whom, and when) as the substance of the obligation, and the invalidating factors (circumstances under which a payment would not count as valid zakat) separately. This framework makes it easy to analyze novel cases: Does this person meet the conditions? Is this payment validly structured? Are there any invalidating factors?

Al-Kasani's treatment of the recipients of zakat in Bada'i as-Sana'i identifies the eight Quranic categories and the conditions for belonging to each. He addresses the question of who qualifies as 'poor' (faqir) versus 'needy' (miskin) — two categories often discussed together but technically distinct — with the Hanafi school's specific definitions based on the proportion of one's assets to one's needs.

For fasting, the framework identifies: conditions for the Ramadan fast to be obligatory (Islam, puberty, sanity, physical ability, residence — not traveling), the pillars of the fast (abstaining from things that break the fast, and the intention), and the acts that invalidate the fast (the muftirat). Al-Kasani's systematic listing of the invalidating acts — organized by whether they require qada' only or qada' plus kaffara — gives students a reliable reference for the consequences of different types of fast-breaking.

Hajj in Bada'i as-Sana'i presents the conditions for the obligation (Islam, puberty, sanity, freedom, financial and physical ability, safe road), the pillars of hajj (ihram, wuquf at Arafah, tawaf al-ifadah), the necessary acts (wajibat, including sa'y), and the invalidating factors. Al-Kasani explains the Hanafi distinction between pillars and necessary acts: omitting a pillar means the hajj is void and must be repeated; omitting a necessary act means the hajj is valid but defective, requiring a compensatory sacrifice (dam).

The systematic framework applied to these three pillars in Bada'i as-Sana'i makes the Hanafi school's positions on these obligations unusually clear. Students who master the conditions-pillars-necessary acts-invalidating factors framework for each pillar have a reliable analytical structure for both understanding the law and applying it to specific cases.

Al-Kasani's achievement in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i is to have organized the Hanafi tradition's vast accumulated learning into a system that is not only comprehensive but genuinely learnable — a contribution to Islamic legal education that has served generations of students from Central Asia to South Asia to the Arab world.
title_id: Zakat, Puasa, dan Haji: Kerangka Sistematis Al-Kasani
content_id:
Perlakuan al-Kasani terhadap zakat, puasa, dan haji dalam Bada'i as-Sana'i menerapkan kerangka syarat-rukun-faktor pembatalnya pada tiga pilar Islam ini, menghasilkan penyajian paling jelas dari posisi-posisi Hanafi tentang topik-topik ini yang tersedia dalam literatur klasik. Bagian-bagian zakat terutama kuat: al-Kasani mendefinisikan syarat-syarat kewajiban zakat (nisab, haul, kepemilikan penuh), mengidentifikasi kategori-kategori kekayaan yang wajib dizakati, menetapkan tarif-tarif yang berlaku, dan mempresentasikan posisi-posisi Hanafi tentang penerima zakat yang sah, semuanya dalam kerangka sistematis yang sama.

Bagian-bagian puasa menerapkan kerangka pada kewajiban dan sunnah puasa Ramadan, kondisi-kondisi yang membatalkan puasa, dan hukum-hukum kafarah untuk berbagai pelanggaran. Al-Kasani memberikan perhatian khusus pada pertanyaan-pertanyaan di mana kondisi-kondisi khusus — perjalanan, sakit, kehamilan, menyusui — memengaruhi kewajiban puasa, dan penanganannya terhadap pertanyaan-pertanyaan ini terperinci dan praktis.

Bagian-bagian haji menunjukkan kerangka pada kompleksitas terbesar, karena ibadah haji adalah yang paling elaborat dari pilar-pilar Islam dalam hal kebutuhan untuk spesifikasi kondisi dan urutan. Penyajian al-Kasani tentang syarat-syarat wajibnya haji, rukun-rukun esensialnya, perbuatan-perbuatan yang diperlukan, dan konsekuensi-konsekuensi dari berbagai pelanggaran atau kelalaian bersifat menyeluruh dan telah menjadi kerangka referensi standar untuk hukum haji Hanafi.
content_ar: Al-Kasani's treatment of الزكاة, الصيام, and الحج in Bada'i as-Sana'i applies his systematic conditions-pillars-invalidating factors framework to these three pillars of الإسلام, producing the clearest available organizational account of الحنفي positions on these foundational obligations. For الزكاة, al-Kasani identifies the conditions that make the الالتزام arise (shurut al-wujub): الإسلام, freedom, sanity, nisab-level الثروة, and the hawl. He then identifies what the الزكاة الالتزام itself consists of (what must be paid, to whom, and عندما) as the substance of the الالتزام, and the invalidating factors (circumstances under which a payment would not count as valid الزكاة) separately. This framework makes it easy to analyze novel cases: Does this person meet the conditions? Is this payment validly structured? Are there any invalidating factors? Al-Kasani's treatment of the recipients of الزكاة in Bada'i as-Sana'i identifies the eight القرآني categories and the conditions for belonging to each. He addresses the question of who qualifies as 'poor' (faqir) versus 'needy' (miskin) — two categories often discussed together but technically distinct — with the الحنفي school's specific definitions based on the proportion of one's assets to one's needs. For الصيام, the framework identifies: conditions for the Ramadan fast to be obligatory (الإسلام, puberty, sanity, physical ability, residence — not traveling), the pillars of the fast (abstaining from things that break the fast, and the intention), and the acts that invalidate the fast (the muftirat). Al-Kasani's systematic listing of the invalidating acts — organized by whether they require القضاء' only or القضاء' plus kaffara — gives students a reliable reference for the consequences of different types of fast-breaking. الحج in Bada'i as-Sana'i presents the conditions for the الالتزام (الإسلام, puberty, sanity, freedom, financial and physical ability, safe road), the pillars of الحج (ihram, wuquf at Arafah, tawaf al-ifadah), the necessary acts (wajibat, بما في ذلك sa'y), and the invalidating factors. Al-Kasani explains the الحنفي distinction between pillars and necessary acts: omitting a pillar means the الحج is void and must be repeated; omitting a necessary act means the الحج is valid but defective, requiring a compensatory sacrifice (dam). The systematic framework applied to these three pillars in Bada'i as-Sana'i makes the الحنفي school's positions on these obligations unusually clear. Students who master the conditions-pillars-necessary acts-invalidating factors framework for each pillar have a reliable analytical structure for both الفهم the law and applying it to specific cases. Al-Kasani's achievement in Bada'i as-Sana'i fi Tartib ash-Shara'i is to have organized the الحنفي التراث's vast accumulated learning into a system that is not only comprehensive but genuinely learnable — a contribution to Islamic legal education that has served generations of s
